lessons.title: ADC Fundamentals at the Register Level
lessons.content:
An ADC maps an analog input onto an $N$-bit integer code. The smallest distinguishable step, one LSB, is

$$V_{LSB}=\frac{V_{ref}}{2^N}$$

so a 12-bit ADC with $V_{ref}=3.3\,\text{V}$ resolves $\approx 0.806\,\text{mV}$. The output code is

$$\text{code}=\left\lfloor \frac{V_{in}}{V_{ref}}\cdot 2^N \right\rfloor$$

clamped to $[0, 2^N-1]$. At the register level you typically configure a **resolution** field (e.g. 12/10/8/6 bits), select the **reference** (an external pin, VDDA, or an on-chip bandgap), and pick the **input channel** in a sequence register.

The front end is a **sample-and-hold (S/H)**: a switch charges a hold capacitor $C_H$ through the source resistance $R_S$ plus the internal switch resistance. The sampling capacitor must settle to within $\tfrac{1}{2}$ LSB, which takes several RC time constants. As a rule of thumb you need

$$t_{samp} \ge (N+1)\ln 2 \cdot R_S C_H$$

so a **high source impedance forces a longer sampling time** (set via the sample-time register) or an op-amp buffer. Skimp on it and you read a value pulled toward the previous channel. This is exactly the radar/instrumentation lesson: characterize your source impedance before trusting a code.

items.front: Write the formula for one LSB of an N-bit ADC and compute it for 12 bits, Vref = 3.3 V.
What does the sample-and-hold (S/H) circuit do, and what limits how fast it can capture?
items.back: A switch charges a hold capacitor C_H so the input is frozen during conversion. Charging time is set by R_S (source) + R_switch through C_H; the cap must settle to <= 1/2 LSB.

items.front: Why does a high source impedance require a longer ADC sampling time?
items.back: Larger R_S increases the RC time constant charging C_H, so more time is needed to settle to within 1/2 LSB. Otherwise charge sharing leaves residual error (often from the previous channel).

items.front: Name three common reference (Vref) sources an MCU ADC can use.
items.back: An external Vref pin, the analog supply VDDA, or an internal bandgap reference. Accuracy of Vref directly scales every conversion.

items.prompt: A 10-bit ADC uses Vref = 2.5 V. What is V_LSB?
You read a sensor with 100 kohm output impedance and codes look wrong, biased toward the previously sampled channel. Best first fix?
choices: Increase the ADC sample-time setting or add a buffer op-amp
items.explanation: Symptom is incomplete S/H settling from high source impedance. Lengthen sample time or buffer the source so C_H fully charges.

items.prompt: Doubling the ADC resolution from 6 to 12 bits, with Vref fixed, changes V_LSB by what factor?
items.explanation: V_LSB ~ 1/2^N. Going from 2^6 to 2^12 multiplies the denominator by 2^6 = 64, so V_LSB shrinks to 1/64.

items.prompt: What does the raw ADC code represent?
choices: An absolute voltage independent of Vref
A ratio of V_in to V_ref scaled by 2^N
items.explanation: code = floor((V_in/V_ref) * 2^N). The conversion is ratiometric to the reference, which is why Vref accuracy matters.

lessons.title: Conversion Modes, Triggering & Throughput
lessons.content:
Once the analog path is sound, you choose **how** conversions are launched. **Single (one-shot)** mode converts once per trigger. **Continuous** mode re-arms itself the instant a conversion finishes, free-running as fast as it can. **Scan/sequence** mode walks an ordered list of channels (regular sequence registers), optionally storing each result via DMA so you can sample, say, four sensors per sweep.

**Triggering** is the crux. **Software trigger** (set the start bit) is convenient but the launch time jitters with whatever the CPU was doing. **Timer (hardware) triggering** ties each conversion to a timer event (e.g. TIM update -> TRGO -> ADC start), giving a hardware-precise, low-jitter sample clock at frequency $f_s$. This is the standard pattern for clean acquisition.

Total conversion time is the sampling window plus the SAR steps:

$$t_{conv}=t_{samp}+t_{SAR}=t_{samp}+\frac{N+\delta}{f_{ADC}}$$

and throughput is $f_{s,max}=1/t_{conv}$.

Don't forget **Nyquist**: to represent a signal of bandwidth $B$ you must sample at $f_s>2B$. Because the ADC samples everything, any energy above $f_s/2$ folds (aliases) back into your band — so the **anti-alias filter is analog, ahead of the ADC**, and cannot be undone in software. A radar tech knows this viscerally: an unfiltered spur at $f_s+f$ lands right on top of your target return.

items.front: Contrast single, continuous, and scan ADC modes.
items.back: Single: one conversion per trigger. Continuous: re-arms automatically and free-runs. Scan/sequence: steps through an ordered channel list each trigger (often into DMA).

items.front: Why is timer triggering preferred over software triggering for signal acquisition?
items.back: A timer launches each conversion on a hardware event (TRGO), giving a precise, low-jitter sample interval independent of CPU activity. Software triggers jitter with code timing, corrupting the time base.
items.hint: Jitter and a clean sample clock.

items.front: State the Nyquist criterion and where the anti-alias filter must live.
items.back: Sample at f_s > 2B for a signal of bandwidth B. The anti-alias filter must be analog, before the ADC; aliasing folds in-band energy and cannot be removed afterward.

items.front: Write conversion time and max throughput in terms of sampling and SAR time.
items.hint: Add the sampling window to the bit-trial time.

items.prompt: An input at 5.2 kHz is sampled at 8 kHz with no anti-alias filter. Where does it appear?
items.explanation: f_s/2 = 4 kHz. 5.2 kHz exceeds Nyquist and folds to |8 - 5.2| = 2.8 kHz, masquerading as a lower-frequency tone.

items.prompt: You need exactly evenly-spaced samples at 50 kHz to feed an FFT. Which trigger source?
choices: Software start in a polling loop
items.explanation: A timer at 50 kHz driving the ADC gives a hardware-locked, uniform sample interval. Software/continuous free-run produce non-uniform spacing that smears the FFT.

items.prompt: If t_samp = 1.5 us and t_SAR = 0.9 us, the maximum single-channel throughput is about:
items.explanation: t_conv = 1.5 + 0.9 = 2.4 us, so f_s,max = 1/2.4 us ~= 417 kSPS.

items.prompt: Continuous conversion mode means the ADC:
choices: Converts once then stops until re-triggered
Re-arms itself and converts back-to-back without a new trigger
Requires a timer event per conversion
items.explanation: Continuous mode automatically restarts after each conversion, free-running at the maximum rate without an external trigger per sample.

lessons.title: Timer+DMA Acquisition, Oversampling, Calibration & DAC/DDS
lessons.content:
The headline pattern: **timer -> ADC -> DMA -> circular buffer**. A timer's TRGO triggers each conversion; the ADC raises a request on end-of-conversion; the DMA copies the data register into RAM and auto-increments. With a **circular (ping-pong/double) buffer** plus **half-transfer (HT)** and **transfer-complete (TC)** interrupts, the CPU processes the half that just filled while the other half fills — an evenly-sampled block, captured with near-zero CPU babysitting, ready for an FFT.

**Oversampling** trades speed for resolution. Averaging (or decimating) $M$ samples of a signal with sufficient noise dither raises the effective number of bits by

$$\Delta N=\frac{1}{2}\log_2 M$$

so $M=256$ buys 4 extra bits. A hardware oversampler does this in the ADC and right-shifts automatically.

**Calibration** corrects **offset** (zero error) and **gain** (full-scale slope) error; many MCUs run a startup self-calibration into a register. **Internal channels** (a die temperature sensor, the bandgap $V_{ref}$) let you measure VDDA and compensate.

The **DAC** is the inverse: code in, voltage out, $V_{out}=\frac{\text{code}}{2^N}V_{ref}$, with an optional output buffer. Drive it from a **timer + DMA** streaming a sine lookup table and you have **DDS**: output frequency $f_{out}=f_{update}/L$ for a table of length $L$. Finally, **SAR vs sigma-delta**: SAR is fast, low-latency, moderate resolution (great for radar/scan); sigma-delta oversamples a 1-bit modulator for very high resolution at low bandwidth (great for audio/precision DC).

items.front: Describe the timer -> ADC -> DMA circular-buffer acquisition chain and the CPU's role.
items.back: Timer TRGO triggers each ADC conversion; on EOC the DMA copies the data register to RAM and increments. A circular double buffer with HT/TC interrupts lets the CPU process the just-filled half while the other fills. CPU does no per-sample work.

items.front: How many extra effective bits does oversampling by M = 256 give, and why?
items.back: $\Delta N = \tfrac{1}{2}\log_2 M = \tfrac{1}{2}\log_2 256 = 4$ bits. Averaging uncorrelated noise reduces it as sqrt(M), gaining 0.5 bit per 4x oversample.

items.front: What are offset and gain calibration, and what are internal channels good for?
items.back: Offset corrects the zero-point error; gain corrects the full-scale slope. Internal channels (temperature sensor, bandgap Vref) let firmware measure die temp and the true VDDA to compensate readings.

items.front: Give the DDS output-frequency relation for a timer+DMA driving a DAC from a length-L sine table.
items.back: $f_{out}=f_{update}/L$, where f_update is the DAC sample rate set by the timer and L is the lookup-table length. Change f_update (or L, or use a phase accumulator) to tune.

items.prompt: Why use half-transfer AND transfer-complete interrupts on a circular ADC-DMA buffer?
choices: To process one half while the other half is still being filled (no overwrite)
items.explanation: HT fires at the midpoint and TC at the end, giving a ping-pong: the CPU consumes the completed half while DMA keeps writing the other, avoiding data races and overwrites.

items.prompt: You oversample by 64x with adequate dither. Effective added resolution is:
For a fast, low-latency, per-sample-converting ADC in a radar/scan front end, which architecture fits best?
items.explanation: SAR gives one accurate conversion per trigger with low latency and good speed - ideal for timer-triggered scanning. Sigma-delta trades latency/bandwidth for very high resolution, better suited to audio/precision DC.

items.prompt: A DAC sine table has L = 100 entries, updated by a timer at 1 MHz via DMA. Output frequency is:
items.explanation: f_out = f_update / L = 1,000,000 / 100 = 10 kHz. One full table traversal is one output period.

items.back: Block diagram: a timer TRGO triggers the ADC sample-and-hold and SAR, which raises an end-of-conversion request to a circular DMA that writes into an N-sample RAM buffer, with half-transfer and transfer-complete interrupts feeding an FFT/radar stage while the CPU stays idle between buffers.
